stories.headline: RSF-linked fighters kill 27 civilians in North Kordofan villages on Eid al-Adha as 19.5M Sudanese face hunger
stories.summary: A Rapid Support Forces-affiliated convoy attacked villages near Bara in Sudan's North Kordofan state on Eid al-Adha, killing at least 27 civilians including elderly residents, per the Sudan Doctors Network. Sudan's Foreign Ministry has called for UN Security Council action. The attack coincided with a UN-backed report that 19.5 million Sudanese — over 40% of the population — are facing acute hunger.

perspectives.keyFraming: Centers Sudanese civilian death tolls and the parallel hunger emergency — US front pages have run zero banner coverage despite UN genocide findings.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera frames the Bara attack as the latest documented mass civilian killing in an RSF campaign already labeled genocidal by UN investigators in El Fasher. The Doctors Network and Sudanese Foreign Ministry are demanding international action, but the West remains largely silent. Coverage emphasizes the convergence of mass atrocity with collapsing food security.

sources.title: Sudan calls for international action after RSF massacre kills dozens in North Kordofan

stories.headline: UN: 30,000 displaced in 10 days as gangs overrun Port-au-Prince and MSF evacuates Cite Soleil hospital
stories.summary: OCHA reports approximately 30,000 people displaced from Port-au-Prince and surrounding areas in 10 days as armed gangs intensified attacks on Cite Soleil and Croix des Bouquets. Doctors Without Borders evacuated its Cite Soleil hospital after sheltering 800 civilians under sustained gunfire. National displacement now exceeds 1.4 million; 5.8 million Haitians (52%) face crisis-level food insecurity. Gangs control roughly 90% of the capital.

perspectives.keyFraming: Frames Haiti's collapse as a humanitarian emergency requiring international protection — not a migration management problem — inverting the US frame.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera and UN News stress the rapid collapse of any remaining state authority in Port-au-Prince, with MSF unable to operate even a single hospital amid gang clashes. The Kenya-led mission has failed to dent gang territorial control. Global South coverage frames this as the world's most ignored humanitarian disaster while Western capitals refuse robust intervention and asylum protections.

sources.title: 30,000 people flee fresh violence in Haiti as hunger crisis deepens

stories.headline: China patrols Scarborough Shoal as Philippines names Beijing as sole obstacle to South China Sea code of conduct
stories.summary: China's PLA Navy and Coast Guard conducted patrols at Scarborough Shoal on May 31, hours after Philippine Defence Secretary Gilberto Teodoro Jnr used the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore to brand Beijing the 'counterparty' blocking an ASEAN code of conduct. Manila is investigating reports of new Chinese structures at the shoal, days after the first US Coast Guard vessel joined Philippine forces in a joint patrol.

perspectives.keyFraming: SCMP frames this as an ASEAN diplomatic crisis where Manila publicly cornered Beijing on the world stage — US wires reduce SCS flashpoints to bilateral US-China posturing.
perspectives.summary: South China Morning Post reports Teodoro stunned Shangri-La by openly singling out China as the lone obstacle to an SCS code of conduct ASEAN has chased since 2002. Beijing responded within hours with patrols and an air-defense interception. Manila is also probing reports of new artificial structures at Scarborough.

sources.title: Philippines says Beijing main hurdle to South China Sea code of conduct

perspectives.keyFraming: Filipino and Japanese coverage centers Manila's investigation into new Chinese structures at Scarborough and the strategic stakes for ASEAN.
perspectives.summary: GMA News and Japan Times treat the Chinese patrol as a direct response to Teodoro's remarks. The reporting emphasizes that if new artificial structures at Scarborough are confirmed, they would mirror China's island-building in the Spratlys and force ASEAN's hand on enforcement of the SCS Tribunal ruling.

sources.title: China patrols Scarborough Shoal after Philippines warns of threat
China patrols Scarborough Shoal after Philippines warns of threat

stories.headline: Nicaragua's Brooklyn Rivera, jailed Miskito Indigenous leader, dies in custody; OAS demands independent probe
stories.summary: Brooklyn Rivera, founder of the Yatama Indigenous-rights movement and longtime advocate for Miskito land claims, died May 31 at age 73 after nearly three years in Ortega-Murillo detention on terrorism charges he denied. The government cited a bacterial infection after COVID-19, but emaciated May 27 hospital photos had already drawn UN, OAS, and Indigenous-rights condemnation. OAS Secretary General called for an 'immediate, independent, transparent investigation.'

perspectives.keyFraming: Centers the Miskito people's autonomous-region land struggle against gold/silver concessions and the Ortega regime's treatment of political prisoners.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera and Havana Times center the Miskito autonomy struggle and Yatama's role in challenging concession-based extractivism in Nicaragua's Caribbean coast. The framing treats Rivera's death as the culmination of a years-long strategy by the Ortega-Murillo government to neutralize Indigenous opposition through detention, with international institutions powerless to enforce earlier human-rights findings.

sources.title: Nicaragua confirms death in custody of Indigenous leader Brooklyn Rivera
Nicaragua's Dictatorship Conceals Death of Brooklyn Rivera

perspectives.keyFraming: Tracks UN human-rights statements and Rivera's biography from Sandinista-era exile to Yatama founding; presents both government and critic accounts of his deterioration.
perspectives.summary: AP via NBC News tracks both the government's account of bacterial infection following COVID-19 and the OAS Secretary General's call for an independent probe. Center coverage treats the May 27 emaciated hospital photos as the trigger that elevated Rivera's case from a domestic detention to an international human-rights incident.

sources.title: Indigenous leader Brooklyn Rivera dies in Nicaragua after nearly 3 years of detention

stories.headline: South Africa xenophobia crisis escalates continent-wide as bodies petition the AU ahead of a vigilante 'June 30' deadline
stories.summary: Anti-migrant violence by groups like Operation Dudula has driven Ghana to evacuate citizens and prompted advisories from Nigeria, Kenya, Malawi, Lesotho and Zimbabwe. On May 31 the Africa Continental Chamber of Producers urged the AU and AfCFTA Secretariat to intervene, as activists set a June 30 deadline for undocumented foreigners to leave. The escalation is largely absent from US front pages.

perspectives.keyFraming: Frames xenophobia as a betrayal of pan-Africanism threatening the AfCFTA free-movement project — an angle US wires treat as isolated local violence rather than a continental-integration crisis.
perspectives.summary: Ghanamma reports the Africa Continental Chamber of Producers raised the alarm on May 31, urging the AU and AfCFTA Secretariat to act and to investigate whether outside actors are sponsoring xenophobic activity to slow continental integration. The appeal follows Ghana's evacuations, an African-ambassador boycott of Africa Day, and South African Tourism conceding cancelled bookings from African source markets.

sources.title: ACCP raises alarm over rising xenophobia in South Africa, calls for intervention of AU and AfCFTA

stories.headline: Nepal PM Balen Shah's claim that Kathmandu also 'encroached' on Indian-claimed land ignites border-dispute row
stories.summary: In his first parliamentary appearance, Nepal's new PM Balendra 'Balen' Shah said the Kalapani-Limpiyadhura-Lipulekh dispute is 'not one-sided' and that Nepal too occupies Indian-claimed territory under the 1816 Sugauli Treaty. The remark broke Nepal's longstanding posture, drew domestic backlash and a Foreign Ministry clarification; India's MEA called the claims 'neither justified nor based on historical facts.' The episode is largely absent from US front pages.

perspectives.keyFraming: Frames a small state asserting even-handedness against a larger neighbor — not the 'snub to India' great-power story US wires would run.
perspectives.summary: Indian and Nepali outlets present Shah's remark as a rupture with Nepal's traditional anti-India framing of the border issue, recasting it as a technical, two-sided demarcation problem under the Sugauli Treaty and a test of Shah's India-China balancing. The comments drew sharp criticism from Nepali nationalists who see them as conceding sovereign claims.

sources.title: PM Balen Shah says Nepal-India border row not one-sided, both 'encroach' on each other's territory
Balen Shah's 'Nepal encroached India's territories' remark triggers row. What did he actually say?

perspectives.keyFraming: Centers the Nepali state's damage control — a technical clarification, not a policy reversal — an angle absent from Western wires entirely.
perspectives.summary: Nepal's Foreign Ministry said Shah referred to localized 'cross-border occupation' identified by a technical committee and did not alter Nepal's sovereign claims to Kalapani, Lipulekh and Limpiyadhura. The clarification sought to calm a domestic uproar while keeping the door open to talks with New Delhi.

sources.title: PM Shah's expression is related to Cross-Border Occupation: Foreign Ministry

stories.headline: Thousands march across Kenya against femicide on Madaraka Day, demanding Ruto declare a national crisis
stories.summary: On June 1, Kenya's Madaraka Day, thousands rallied in Nairobi under the #EndFemicideKE banner, joined by former Chief Justice David Maraga, as police fired tear gas. Protesters demand that femicide be declared a national crisis amid roughly one woman killed per day and thousands of children reported missing. The May 28 Utumishi Girls Academy disaster sharpened the anger, but the mobilization is largely absent from US front pages.

perspectives.keyFraming: Local press centers state failure and the symbolic rebuke of independence-day pageantry — 'not yet freedom' for the nation's women.
perspectives.summary: Kenyan outlets report thousands in Nairobi's central business district demanding stronger enforcement, faster investigations and a national-crisis declaration, with David Maraga among the marchers. Organizers tied the protest to the Utumishi Girls Academy tragedy and a femicide rate near one woman a day, casting Madaraka Day as hollow for Kenyan women.

sources.title: Violent start to #EndFemicideKe demos as police lob tear gas at peaceful protesters
[PHOTOS] Kenyans stage anti-femicide protests in Nairobi

perspectives.keyFraming: State framing pivots to Madaraka Day development pledges and an apology to the marginalized north — changing the subject from femicide.
perspectives.summary: Government-aligned coverage foregrounds the 63rd Madaraka Day held in Wajir — the first ever in northern Kenya — President Ruto's apology for historic marginalization and new development pledges, while sidestepping the simultaneous femicide protests and the demand to declare a national emergency.

sources.title: Madaraka Day celebrations in Wajir highlight push for balanced national development, government says

stories.headline: FARC dissidents declare a return to war in Colombia's Catatumbo as the 2016 peace deal frays
stories.summary: In an exclusive aired May 30, a FARC dissident faction told Al Jazeera it has returned to armed struggle in the Catatumbo region, blaming the failed 2016 peace accord for absent state protection and a security vacuum filled by rival armed groups. The declaration lands as Colombia endures its worst displacement crisis in a decade and as a tense first-round presidential vote heads to a June 21 runoff — a domestic security collapse largely absent from US front pages.

perspectives.keyFraming: US wires cover Colombia only through the presidential horse-race; the missed angle is rebels' own claim that the Nobel-celebrated 2016 deal collapsed because the state never arrived in former FARC strongholds.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera's on-the-ground access lets dissidents frame their re-armament as a response to broken promises — no security, no social investment, and the killing of demobilized fighters and community leaders. The framing is a structural indictment of Colombia's peace implementation rather than a simple crime story, centering the abandoned periphery of the conflict.

sources.title: Al Jazeera exclusive interview with rebel FARC faction in Colombia

perspectives.keyFraming: US pages frame the May 31 vote mainly as a future US-relations question; the missed angle is the link between security collapse in regions like Catatumbo and the law-and-order surge that pushed the far right to a runoff.
perspectives.summary: France 24's election coverage situates the violence and rebel resurgence as the backdrop to a polarized runoff between far-right outsider Abelardo de la Espriella and leftist Iván Cepeda, where insecurity drove an unexpectedly strong law-and-order vote. The angle is a domestic Colombian dynamic distinct from the US-centric framing dominant in American outlets.

sources.title: Colombia's presidential race goes to a runoff: De la Espriella vs. Cepeda
